title: Shadows of the Knight - Episode 2
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: The C++ proposal is simultaneously the recommended, runtime-focused, and memory-conscious solution. No separate coincident solution-runtime.cpp or solution-memory.cpp files are emitted.

variants.languageReason: C++ gives native integer arithmetic for exact squared-distance comparisons, deterministic low-latency interactive I/O, compact vectors for the clipped polygon and candidate sets, and no garbage-collector pauses under the 150 ms per-turn limit.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It combines native speed, exact 64-bit arithmetic, low-overhead geometry containers, and straightforward CodinGame interactive output flushing.
Considered for minimal runtime overhead, but manual vector management for polygon clipping and candidate probes would increase implementation risk without meaningful memory or speed gains over C++.

Considered for native speed and safety. It is competitive, but the borrow/indexing ceremony is less ergonomic for an interactive geometry solver and does not improve the asymptotic or constant-factor profile here.
Considered for compiled performance, but runtime overhead, bounds checks, and garbage-collected slices are less attractive than C++ vectors for top-percentile latency.
Managed runtimes can solve the puzzle, but VM startup, object layout, and possible GC pauses are weaker for strict per-turn interactive performance than compact native C++.
Useful for correctness mirrors and quick experimentation, but interpreter/VM overhead is a poor fit for repeated candidate scoring and geometry updates under a 150 ms turn budget.

variants.approachJustification: Each feedback response is converted into the exact perpendicular-bisector constraint between the previous and current jumps. The possible bomb region is maintained as a clipped convex polygon until exact integer candidate enumeration becomes affordable; from that point, the integer candidate set is filtered directly after every response, also removing the current window as a known miss. The next jump mirrors the current position through the candidate center, giving a binary-search-like cut, and explicit-candidate ties prefer real candidate windows near the candidate center so the endgame wins directly instead of only extracting one more split.
variants.timeComplexity: O(V + A*C + P*K) per turn, where V is the polygon vertex count, C is the number of constraints, A is the capped scanned box area, K is the enumerated candidate count, and P is the capped probe count
variants.spaceComplexity: O(V + C + K), with V and C bounded by the jump count and K capped by either a small box scan or one grid line

reasoningSummary: A WARMER/COLDER/SAME answer compares two squared Euclidean distances. Expanding the squares removes quadratic terms and leaves one linear half-plane or line constraint. Repeated clipping plus center-reflection turns the large implicit grid into a small convex search problem.
timeComplexity: O(V + A*C + P*K) per turn with fixed caps under the CodinGame limits
